his_1.title: Ancient India: Indus Valley Civilization & Vedic Period

subtopics.name: Harappan Urban Planning & Material Culture
bullets: Advanced Urban Grid System: Characterized by an organized grid iron street layout, standardized burnt bricks (ratio 4:2:1), and covered brick-lined drainage networks running along main roads.

High-Yield Site Artifacts: 1. Lothal: An artificial brick **dockyard basin** connected to the tidal waters of the Bhogava river, showing maritime trade. 2. Dholavira: Features a unique **three-tier town configuration** (Citadel, Middle Town, Lower Town) and a sophisticated water management network with large rock-cut reservoirs. 3. Mohenjo-daro: Location of the Great Bath, the bronze Dancing Girl statue (lost-wax technique), and the steatite Bearded Priest.

Metallurgical & Script Boundaries: The Indus Valley was a Bronze Age civilization that mastered copper and bronze work, but **iron was entirely unknown** to them. Their pictographic script remains undeciphered and utilized alternating line directions (Boustrophedon).

subtopics.name: Rig-Vedic vs Later-Vedic Societal Evolution
bullets: Early Vedic Pastorals (1500–1000 BCE): Centered in the Sapta-Sindhu region. Primarily a pastoral, semi-nomadic society where the chief wealth was cows (Gau), and tribal assemblies like the Sabha, Samiti, and Vidatha exercised democratic control over the tribal chief (Rajan). Status of women was high, with participation in assemblies.

Later Vedic Sedentarization (1000–600 BCE): Marked by the migration eastward into the Gangetic plains, driven by the **discovery and mastery of iron (Shyama Ayas)**. Society transitioned to settled agriculture, tribal assemblies lost power to territorial monarchies (Janapadas), and the flexible class structure hardened into a hereditary, birth-based **Chaturvarnya system**.

subtopics.name: Buddhist Doctrines, Literature & Council Matrix
bullets: Core Doctrines: Founded on the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path (Ashtangika Marga). Rejects the absolute authority of the Vedas and the efficacy of priestly rituals. Operates on **Pratityasamutpada** (the doctrine of dependent origination/cause-and-effect).

The Canonical Tipitaka: 1. Vinaya Pitaka: Rules and regulations for monastic discipline. 2. Sutta Pitaka: Collected discourses and sermons of the Buddha. 3. Abhidhamma Pitaka: Philosophical interpretation and analysis of doctrines.

Sect Splits & Councils (High-Yield): 1. First Council: Held at Rajgriha under King Ajatashatru. 2. Third Council: Held at Pataliputra under Ashoka, where the Abhidhamma was compiled. 3. Fourth Council: Held in Kashmir under **Kanishka**, causing the final split into **Hinayana** (orthodox, treats Buddha as a guide) and **Mahayana** (deifies Buddha, introduces Bodhisattva worship and Sanskrit canons).

subtopics.name: Jain Philosophy, Tirthankaras & Canonical Splits
bullets: Core Epistemology: Anchored by Vardhamana Mahavira (the 24th Tirthankara). Rejects the creator God concept and posits that every living and non-living entity possesses a soul (Jiva). Features two major philosophical tenets: 1. Anekantavada: The doctrine of the multiplicity of viewpoints. 2. Syadvada: The relativity of all knowledge.

Ascetic Vows & Sect Splits: Prescribes five great vows (Mahavratas): Ahimsa (extreme non-violence), Satya, Asteya, Aparigraha (non-possession), and Brahmacharya (added by Mahavira). Split into **Digambaras** (sky-clad, led by Bhadrabahu, orthodox) and **Svetambaras** (white-clad, led by Sthulabhadra) during a severe Magadha famine.

his_3.title: Mauryan Empire, Ashokan Edicts & Administrative Machinery

subtopics.name: Bureaucratic Structure & Epigraphic Proclamations
bullets: Kautilyan Saptanga Theory: Defines the state as an organic body with 7 limbs: Swami (King), Amatya (Bureaucracy), Janapada (Territory/Population), Durga (Fort), Kosa (Treasury), Danda (Army), and Mitra (Ally).

Ashokan Epigraphy: Edicts were written primarily in the **Prakrit language using the Brahmi script** in mainland India. In the northwestern frontiers, they utilized the Kharosthi script (written left-to-right), and in Afghanistan, they used Aramaic and Greek scripts. **James Prinsep** deciphered them in 1837.

Major Rock Edicts (High-Yield): 1. Major Rock Edict XIII: Describes the Kalinga War devastation and Ashoka's change of heart from Bherighosha (war drum) to Dhammaghosha (peace/righteousness). 2. Minor Rock Edict: Explicitly contains his personal name 'Ashoka' at Maski, Netter, and Gujjarra (otherwise he used his titles *Devanampiya Piyadasi*).

his_4.title: Gupta Empire & The Classical Age (Art, Literature & Science)

subtopics.name: Land Grants, Literature, and Scientific Frontiers
bullets: The Rise of Land Grants (Agrahara): Characterized by the extensive institutionalization of tax-free land grants to Brahmins and temples. These grants included administrative and judicial immunities, which accelerated political decentralization and laid the groundwork for Indian feudalism.

Sanskrit Literature Peak: Supported by royal patronage (like Chandragupta II's Navaratnas). Kalidasa compiled masterworks like Abhijnanasakuntalam, and Vishnusharma wrote the Panchatantra. **UPSC Trap:** While religious texts like the Puranas were finalized, secular plays showed a social hierarchy: upper-caste males spoke refined Sanskrit, while women and lower-caste characters spoke Prakrit dialects.

Scientific Frontiers: Aryabhata authored the Aryabhatiya, describing the heliocentric rotation of the earth, calculating the exact value of $\pi$, and explaining solar/lunar eclipses. Varahamihira authored the Brihat Samhita, an encyclopedia of astronomy and geography.

his_5.title: Major Dynasties of South India (Cholas, Pallavas, Chalukyas & Rashtrakutas)

subtopics.name: Imperial Chola Administration & Rock-Cut Pallava Shrines
bullets: Chola Local Self-Government: Celebrated for its village assemblies described in the **Uttaramerur Inscriptions** (919 and 921 CE). The village was managed by two assemblies: the Ur (general assembly) and the Sabha/Mahasabha (exclusive assembly of Brahmins). Members were selected via a lottery system (Kudavolai) from wards, subject to strict property and educational qualifications.

Dravidian Architecture Peak: Raja Raja I constructed the **Brihadisvara Temple at Thanjavur**, featuring a massive monolithic stone vimana tower. Chola artisans perfected the production of **lost-wax bronze Nataraja statues** representing Shiva's cosmic dance.

Pallava Rock-Cut Evolution: Pioneered rock-cut temple engineering under Narasimhavarman I, who built the **Pancha Rathas** (monolithic rock temples) and the open-air relief 'Descent of the Ganges' at Mahabalipuram.

his_6.title: Delhi Sultanate - Slave, Khalji, Tughlaq Dynasties & Administration

subtopics.name: Iqta Mechanics & Alauddin Khalji's Price Controls
bullets: The Iqta System: Institutionalized by Iltutmish. Territorial land revenues were assigned to military commanders (Iqtadars/Muqtis) in lieu of cash salaries. Iqtadars were responsible for collecting land revenue, maintaining a standing army contingency for the Sultan, and **did not possess permanent ownership rights** over the land.

Alauddin Khalji's Market Reforms: Implemented strict price controls on essential commodities in Delhi to maintain a large standing army on low cash wages. He set up three independent markets (grain, cloth, horses/cattle) overseen by intelligence inspectors known as Shahna-i-Mandi.

Muhammad bin Tughlaq's Policy Failures: 1. Capital Shift: Moved the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (Devagiri) to better manage South India, but had to reverse it due to defense vulnerabilities against Mongol incursions. 2. Token Currency: Introduced copper/brass token coins at par value with silver tankas, which failed completely due to widespread illegal private minting.

his_7.title: Mughal Empire - Policies, Mansabdari System & Art/Architecture

subtopics.name: The Mansabdari-Zabt Framework & Indo-Islamic Aesthetics
bullets: The Mansabdari System: The unique administrative and military structure established by Akbar. Every officer was assigned a rank (Mansab) divided into two numerical scales: 1. Zat: Determined the officer's personal status, position in the court hierarchy, and salary scale. 2. Sawar: Indicated the exact number of cavalrymen the officer was legally required to maintain.

Zabt and Dahsala Revenue Systems: Engineered by Raja Todar Mal. It involved the detailed survey and measurement of agricultural lands, followed by calculating the average yield and prices of crops over the **past 10 years (Dahsala)**. One-third of the average produce was collected as the state tax share, settled primarily in cash.

Mughal Architectural Evolution: Transitioned from red sandstone under Akbar (Fatehpur Sikri, Buland Darwaza) to white marble under Shah Jahan. Introduced the **Pietra Dura** technique (inlaying polished semi-precious colored stones into marble walls) and built magnificent structures featuring central bulbous domes and symmetrical minarets (Taj Mahal).

subtopics.name: Bhakti Dualism, Sufi Silsilahs & Nayaka Administration
bullets: Bhakti Philosophical Splits: 1. Saguna Bhakti: Worshipped God in a personalized, anthropomorphic form with distinct attributes (e.g., Tulsidas, Chaitanya, Mirabai). 2. Nirguna Bhakti: Worshipped an abstract, formless, omnipresent reality without physical attributes (e.g., Kabir, Guru Nanak).

Sufi Order (Silsilah) Dynamics: 1. Chishti Silsilah: Established in India by Moinuddin Chishti; emphasized simple living, spiritual music (Sama), and **stayed away from state politics**. 2. Suhrawardi Silsilah: Accepted state appointments, accumulated wealth, and operated primarily in Punjab and Sindh.

Vijayanagara Empire (1336–1646): Founded by Harihara and Bukka of the Sangama dynasty. Reached its peak under **Krishnadevarva** (Tuluva dynasty), who authored the Telugu classic Amuktamalyada. Developed the **Ayagar and Nayaka systems**, where military chiefs (Nayakas) were granted land revenues (Amaram) in exchange for maintaining military forces for the King. Visited by foreign travelers like Nicolo de Conti, Abdur Razzaq, and Domingo Paes.

his_9.title: British Expansion - Battle of Plassey, Buxar & Subsidiary Alliance

subtopics.name: Military Engagements & Imperial Land Alliances
bullets: Battle of Plassey (1757): Robert Clive defeated Nawab Siraj-ud-Daulah of Bengal through the defection of military commander Mir Jafar. It marked the EIC's transition from a purely commercial entity to a political kingmaker in India.

Battle of Buxar (1764): The combined forces of Mir Qasim (Bengal), Shuja-ud-Daulah (Awadh), and Shah Alam II (Mughal Emperor) were defeated by Hector Munro's British troops. This led to the **Treaty of Allahabad (1765)**, which granted the East India Company the **Diwani Rights** (the right to collect civil revenue) over Bengal, Bihar, and Odisha.

Subsidiary Alliance System: Perfected by **Lord Wellesley**. Indian rulers who entered the alliance had to permanently disband their own armies and maintain a British contingent within their territory at their own expense. They also had to station a British Resident at their court and could not employ any other European or enter into foreign alliances without EIC consent. **Hyderabad** was the first state to accept it (1798).

Doctrine of Lapse: Implemented by **Lord Dalhousie**. It dictated that if an Indian ruler of a dependent state died without a natural male heir, the state was automatically annexed by the British Crown; adopted heirs were not recognized. Used to annex Satara (1848), Sambalpur, Jhansi (1853), and Nagpur. **Trap:** Awadh was annexed in 1856 on charges of **misgovernance**, not the Doctrine of Lapse.

his_10.title: Economic Impact of British Rule & Land Revenue Systems

subtopics.name: Permanent Settlement, Ryotwari, Mahalwari & Deindustrialization
bullets: Permanent Settlement (Bengal, 1793): Introduced by Lord Cornwallis. **Zamindars were recognized as absolute owners of the land**. The land revenue demand was fixed permanently, and if a Zamindar failed to pay by sunset on a specified day, their land was auctioned off (**Sunset Law**). The state received $\frac{10}{11}$ of the revenue, leaving $\frac{1}{11}$ for the Zamindar.

Ryotwari Settlement (Madras/Bombay): Developed by Thomas Munro and Captain Alexander Read. Settlements were made **directly with the individual cultivators (Ryots)** without intermediate Zamindars. The revenue was fixed for 20-30 years based on soil productivity and could be revised upward, with the tax rate set around 50-60%.

Mahalwari Settlement (North-West Provinces): Engineered by Holt Mackenzie. The revenue settlement was made collectively with the **village community or estate (Mahal)**. Village headmen (Lambardars) were responsible for collecting the tax share.

Economic Drain Theory: Popularized by **Dadabhai Naoroji** in his book Poverty and Un-British Rule in India (1901). He demonstrated that Britain was systematically extracting unilateral wealth and resources out of India without equivalent economic returns, using instruments like 'Home Charges' and public debt balances.

subtopics.name: The Sepoy Mutiny & Modern Rationalist Reforms
bullets: The Revolt of 1857 Mechanics: Sparked at Meerut by sepoys protesting the introduction of Enfield rifles with greased cartridges. It spread rapidly across northern India, drawing in displaced rulers like Nana Sahib, Tantia Tope, and Rani Laxmibai. **UPSC Trap:** The revolt remained geographically limited, failing to gain traction in southern India, and was **opposed by western-educated middle classes** and major princely rulers.

Raja Ram Mohan Roy & Brahmo Samaj: Founded the Brahmo Samaj in 1828 to promote rationalism, monotheism, and human dignity, while campaigning against idolatry and polygamy. His persistent efforts led to the **Abolition of Sati Regulation in 1829** by Governor-General Lord William Bentinck.

Key Reformers to Memorize: 1. Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar: His tireless legal advocacy led to the passage of the **Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act, 1856**. 2. Jyotirao Phule: Founded the **Satyashodhak Samaj** (1873) in Maharashtra to challenge caste oppression and champion anti-priestly education for lower castes. 3. Swami Dayanand Saraswati: Founded the Arya Samaj (1875), coined the slogan **'Back to the Vedas'**, and rejected hereditary caste hierarchies.

his_12.title: Indian National Movement - Moderate, Extremist & Gandhian Phases

phases.phaseName: The Struggle for Swaraj & Mass Mobilization

subtopics.name: Moderate Politics vs Swadeshi Surge & Gandhian Satyagrahas
bullets: Indian National Congress Foundation (1885): Founded at Bombay by retired British civil servant **Allan Octavian Hume**. The first official session was presided over by Womesh Chandra Bonnerjee.

Moderate Phase (1885–1905): Led by leaders like Dadabhai Naoroji, Pherozeshah Mehta, and Gopal Krishna Gokhale. They utilized constitutional methods summarized as the **3 Ps: Petition, Prayer, and Protest**, focusing on highlighting colonial economic exploitation.

The Swadeshi Movement (1905–1908): Launched to protest **Lord Curzon's Partition of Bengal** along communal lines. It introduced mass political tactics like boycotting British goods, promoting national education, and fostering indigenous industries. Led by Extremist leaders like Lal, Bal, and Pal (Lala Lajpat Rai, Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Bipin Chandra Pal). It culminated in the **Surat Split of 1907**, where Moderates expelled the Extremists from the INC.

The Gandhian Mass Satygraha Movements: 1. Non-Cooperation Movement (1920–1922): Integrated with the Khilafat movement to build cross-community mobilization against the Rowlatt Act. Abruptly suspended by Gandhi following the outbreak of violence at the **Chauri Chaura incident**. 2. Civil Disobedience Movement (1930): Initiated by the **Dandi Salt March** to break the state salt monopoly. 3. Quit India Movement (1942): Launched during WWII following the failure of the Cripps Mission. Gandhi gave the call **'Do or Die'**, leading to instant preemptive arrests under Operation Zero Hour and sparking a widespread, decentralized popular uprising.

subtopics.name: The Patel-Menon Plan & Tribal Accessions
bullets: The Integration Crucible: At independence, India was fragmented into British provinces and over 562 princely states. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel (States Minister) and V.P. Menon (Secretary) orchestrated their integration using a mix of diplomacy, geopolitical pressure, and the Instrument of Accession.

The Three Holdout States (High-Yield): 1. Junagadh: The Muslim Nawab desired accession to Pakistan despite a majority Hindu population; integrated in late 1947 following a popular uprising and a subsequent **Plebiscite** that voted overwhelmingly for India. 2. Hyderabad: The Nizam attempted to remain independent or join Pakistan, unleashing militant 'Razakars' on the population; integrated in September 1948 via a decisive 5-day military operation code-named Operation Polo. 3. Jammu & Kashmir: Maharaja Hari Singh signed the Instrument of Accession on **October 26, 1947**, only after tribal Pathan raiders backed by the Pakistani army invaded the state, prompting immediate Indian military intervention.

subtopics.name: Commission Timelines & The 1956 Structural Overhaul
bullets: The Dhar Commission (1948): Appointed by the Government to examine the demand for linguistic states; it strongly **rejected language as the primary basis** for redrawing borders, recommending administrative convenience instead.

The JVP Committee (1948): Comprised Jawaharlal Nehru, Vallabhbhai Patel, and Pattabhi Sitaramayya. It also formally **rejected language as a basis for state creation**, fearing it would threaten national security and unity in the wake of partition.

The Potti Sriramulu Catalyst (1952): Following a historic 56-day hunger strike unto death by Telugu activist Potti Sriramulu, intense public pressure forced the government to concede, creating the **first linguistic state: Andhra State** in October 1953 (carved out of Madras province).

Fazl Ali Commission & State Reorganisation Act (1956): A 3-member States Reorganisation Commission (SRC) comprising Fazl Ali, K.M. Panikkar, and H.N. Kunzru. It **accepted language as a broad basis** for reorganization but rejected the 'one language, one state' theory. This directly led to the States Reorganisation Act of 1956, which abolished the old complex Part A, B, C, D state categories and established a clean matrix of 14 States and 6 Union Territories.
